Declaration of Independence|Opening: When you break up with a government, you owe the world an explanation. That is the whole purpose of this document: to state the reasons in public.
Declaration of Independence|Preamble: The core claim: everyone is born with rights that cannot be taken away, governments exist only to protect those rights, and they govern only with the people's consent. When a government destroys those rights instead, the people may replace it. Not for small annoyances, but after a long pattern of abuse.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 1: The king vetoed laws the colonies needed, even beneficial and urgent ones.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 2: He blocked colonial governors from passing urgent laws without his personal approval, then never got around to approving them.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 3: He refused to pass laws for whole regions unless the people there gave up their right to elected representation.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 4: He forced legislatures to meet in strange, distant, uncomfortable places, purely to exhaust them into giving him what he wanted.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 5: He repeatedly shut down elected assemblies whenever they stood up to him.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 6: After dissolving those assemblies, he refused to allow new elections, leaving the colonies without working government and exposed to danger.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 7: He tried to choke off the colonies' growth by blocking immigration and making it hard for newcomers to become citizens or get land.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 8: He blocked the courts from functioning by refusing to approve laws that would establish judicial systems.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 9: He made judges depend on him personally for their jobs and their pay, which meant no colonist could count on a fair ruling against the crown.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 10: He created a swarm of new government offices and sent officials to harass the colonists and drain their resources.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 11: He stationed permanent armies in the colonies during peacetime, without asking their legislatures.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 12: He put the military above civilian government. This grievance is why the Constitution later made a civilian, the President, commander in chief.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 13: He teamed up with Parliament to impose rules the colonial constitutions never agreed to. This line introduces the list of specific offenses that follows.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 14: Colonists were forced to house and feed British soldiers.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 15: Soldiers who killed colonists were protected by sham trials and never truly punished.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 16: Britain cut the colonies off from trading with the rest of the world.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 17: The famous one: taxation without consent. The colonists had no vote in the Parliament that taxed them.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 18: Colonists were denied trial by jury, a right the Bill of Rights later locked in twice.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 19: Colonists could be shipped overseas to face trial far from home, on invented charges.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 20: The king scrapped self-rule in neighboring Quebec and set up arbitrary government there. The colonists read it as a rehearsal for doing the same to them.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 21: He revoked colonial charters, the founding legal agreements, and rewrote how the colonies were governed without their consent.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 22: He suspended elected legislatures and claimed the power to make any law for the colonies, on any subject, from London.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 23: By declaring the colonies outside his protection and making war on them, the king effectively quit being their government.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 24: British forces raided the coasts, burned towns, and killed colonists. This is a wartime bill of particulars, not a policy complaint.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 25: He hired foreign mercenaries, the Hessians, to finish the war against his own subjects.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 26: American sailors captured at sea were forced to fight against their own neighbors or be killed.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 27: He stirred up violence inside the colonies and warfare on the frontier. The passage's language about Native Americans is ugly and reflects the prejudices of 1776; honest readers should see both the grievance and the blind spot.
Declaration of Independence|Grievance 28: The closing argument of the list: the colonists petitioned peacefully, over and over, and every petition was answered with more injury. A ruler who behaves this way is a tyrant unfit to govern free people.
Declaration of Independence|Body: Before separating, the colonists also appealed directly to the British people, not just the king, and were ignored. So Britain gets the same status as everyone else: enemies in war, friends in peace.
Declaration of Independence|Conclusion &: The actual declaration: the colonies are now free and independent states with full power to wage war, make peace, and trade. The signers staked their lives, their fortunes, and their honor on it. This was sign-off with real consequences; a signature here was treason against the crown.
U.S. Constitution|Preamble: The mission statement. Authority comes from 'We the people,' not a king, and the goals are listed up front: justice, peace at home, defense, general welfare, and liberty that lasts beyond one generation.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 1: All federal lawmaking power goes to Congress, split into two chambers: the House and the Senate.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 2: The House of Representatives: elected directly by the people every two years, seats divided by population, and the sole power to impeach. This section also contains the three-fifths clause, the Constitution's gravest compromise, later erased by amendment.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 3: The Senate: two senators per state serving six-year terms, and the court for impeachment trials. Originally senators were chosen by state legislatures; the Seventeenth Amendment gave that choice to voters.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 4: States run their own elections for Congress, but Congress can step in and change the rules. A shared-control design.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 5: Each chamber referees itself: it judges its members' elections, sets its own rules, keeps a public journal, and can expel a member with a two-thirds vote.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 6: Members of Congress get paid by the treasury, are protected from arrest for what they say in debate, and cannot simultaneously hold another federal office. Separation of powers applied to individual careers.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 7: How a bill becomes law: money bills start in the House, everything passes both chambers, the President can veto, and Congress can override with two-thirds of each chamber.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 8: Congress's power list: taxes, borrowing, regulating commerce, immigration, money, post offices, patents and copyrights, courts, declaring war, and raising the army and navy, capped by the 'necessary and proper' clause that lets Congress do what it takes to execute these powers.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 9: Limits on Congress: habeas corpus cannot be casually suspended, no punishment-by-legislation, no titles of nobility. It also barred Congress from touching the slave trade before 1808, a compromise that reads today as the ugliest line in the document.
U.S. Constitution|Article I, Section 10: Limits on the states: no treaties, no printing money, no laws breaking contracts, no import taxes without consent of Congress. One country, one foreign policy, one currency.
U.S. Constitution|Article II, Section 1: The presidency: executive power vested in one person, elected to a four-year term through the Electoral College, who must swear to preserve, protect, and defend the Constitution.
U.S. Constitution|Article II, Section 2: The President commands the military, grants pardons, and makes treaties and top appointments, but the big ones need Senate approval. Power, with a co-signer required.
U.S. Constitution|Article II, Section 3: The President must report to Congress on the state of the union and, crucially, 'take care that the laws be faithfully executed.' The job is to run the laws, not to be above them.
U.S. Constitution|Article II, Section 4: The eject button: the President, Vice President, and civil officers can all be removed for treason, bribery, or other high crimes and misdemeanors.
U.S. Constitution|Article III, Section 1: One Supreme Court, plus lower courts as Congress creates them. Judges serve for life during good behavior with salaries that cannot be cut, so no politician can punish them for a ruling.
U.S. Constitution|Article III, Section 2: What federal courts can hear: cases under the Constitution, federal law, treaties, and disputes between states. All criminal trials get juries, held where the crime happened.
U.S. Constitution|Article III, Section 3: Treason is defined narrowly on purpose: only levying war or aiding enemies, and it takes two witnesses or a confession to convict. The framers had seen 'treason' stretched to silence critics and closed that door.
U.S. Constitution|Article IV, Section 1: States must honor each other's laws, records, and court judgments. Your marriage, your contracts, and your court wins travel with you.
U.S. Constitution|Article IV, Section 2: Citizens of one state get the same basic privileges in every state, and fugitives are returned for trial. This section also held the fugitive slave clause, nullified by the Thirteenth Amendment.
U.S. Constitution|Article IV, Section 3: How new states join the union, on equal footing, with Congress's approval, and Congress governs the territories.
U.S. Constitution|Article IV, Section 4: The federal government guarantees every state a republican form of government, meaning representative, not royal, and protection against invasion.
U.S. Constitution|Article V: The amendment process, the changelog mechanism: two-thirds of Congress (or a convention called by two-thirds of the states) proposes, and three-quarters of the states must ratify. Used 27 times in 250 years. Hard enough to be stable, possible enough to fix grave errors.
U.S. Constitution|Article VI: Old debts stay valid, the Constitution and federal law are the supreme law of the land, every official swears to the Constitution, and no religious test can ever be required for office.
U.S. Constitution|Article VII: The launch condition: ratification by nine of the thirteen states put the Constitution into effect. It did not wait for unanimity.
Bill of Rights|Amendment I: The big five freedoms in one sentence: religion (no state church, free exercise), speech, press, peaceful assembly, and petitioning the government about grievances. Note who it binds: 'Congress shall make no law.' It limits the government, not you.
Bill of Rights|Amendment II: The right to keep and bear arms, framed around the need for a well-regulated militia in a free state. What that means in practice is still the most argued sentence in American law.
Bill of Rights|Amendment III: No forcing homeowners to house soldiers in peacetime. A direct answer to a specific grievance against the king from 1776.
Bill of Rights|Amendment IV: No unreasonable searches or seizures of your person, home, papers, or belongings. Warrants require probable cause, an oath, and must name the specific place and things to be searched. The government cannot go fishing.
Bill of Rights|Amendment V: The accused person's shield: serious charges need a grand jury, no double jeopardy, no forced self-incrimination ('pleading the fifth'), no losing life, liberty, or property without due process, and no property taken for public use without fair payment.
Bill of Rights|Amendment VI: If you are charged with a crime: a speedy public trial, an impartial local jury, the right to know the charges, to confront witnesses, to compel witnesses in your favor, and to have a lawyer.
Bill of Rights|Amendment VII: Jury trials extend to civil lawsuits too, not just criminal cases.
Bill of Rights|Amendment VIII: No excessive bail, no excessive fines, no cruel and unusual punishments. Punishment has a ceiling.
Bill of Rights|Amendment IX: Listing some rights does not mean the people gave up the unlisted ones. The Bill of Rights is a floor, not a complete inventory.
Bill of Rights|Amendment X: Any power not given to the federal government, and not denied to the states, stays with the states or the people. The default owner of power is not Washington.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 1: Madison opens with the problem: factions, groups pursuing their own interest at everyone else's expense, are the disease that killed earlier republics, and a well-built union is the cure.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 2: His definition: a faction is any group, majority or minority, united by a passion or interest that runs against other citizens' rights or the common good.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 3: Factions are not a bug you can patch out; they grow from human nature itself, and the most durable source is the unequal distribution of property. People with different stakes see the world differently.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 4: Nobody should judge their own case, yet lawmaking is exactly that: parties deciding questions they have a stake in. So the system must assume bias rather than hope for fairness.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 5: Do not build a system that requires wise leaders to function. 'Enlightened statesmen will not always be at the helm' is the eighteenth-century version of designing for failure.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 6: Two ways to handle faction: remove its causes or control its effects. Removing causes means destroying liberty or forcing everyone to think alike, a cure worse than the disease. So: control the effects.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 7: The answer is a republic: decisions filtered through elected representatives rather than made by the crowd directly, which can refine public views and scale to a large country.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 8: Representation has ratios that matter: large republics give better odds of electing fit leaders and make it harder for corrupt tactics to work.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 9: The counterintuitive move: bigger is safer. More citizens and more territory mean more competing interests, making it far harder for any one majority to unite and oppress the rest.
Federalist No. 10 (Madison)|Passage 10: A fire of factional rage may burn through one state, but a large federal union keeps it from spreading to the whole country. Blast-radius containment, 1787 edition.
Federalist No. 51 (Madison)|Passage 1: The famous design principle: keep the branches of government structurally independent so each can defend itself. The people are the ultimate source of power for all of them.
Federalist No. 51 (Madison)|Passage 2: The most quoted line in the paper: 'Ambition must be made to counteract ambition.' Government must be designed for real, self-interested humans, because 'men are not angels.' Give each office the means and the motive to resist encroachment by the others.
Federalist No. 51 (Madison)|Passage 3: The double security: power divided twice, once between federal and state governments, and again among branches within each. Rival interests standing guard where good motives cannot be trusted.
Federalist No. 51 (Madison)|Passage 4: Big republics protect minorities better: with so many different interests, an unjust majority coalition is hard to assemble. Justice is the end of government, and this structure is how you get it from imperfect people.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 1: Hamilton turns to the courts: how judges are appointed, how long they serve, and why lifetime tenure during good behavior is the right design.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 2: The judiciary is 'the least dangerous' branch: it commands no army and controls no money, only judgment. Precisely because it is weak, it needs strong independence to survive the other branches.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 3: In a limited constitution, courts must be able to declare laws void when they violate it. Without that power, all the written limits on government 'amount to nothing.' This is the seed of judicial review.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 4: Congress cannot be the judge of its own powers. The Constitution is fundamental law, it belongs to the people, and courts exist to keep the legislature inside it.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 5: Striking down a law does not make judges superior to Congress; it means the people, whose Constitution outranks any statute, are superior to both.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 6: When a fundamental law and an ordinary law conflict, the fundamental one wins, the same way a principal outranks an agent.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 7: Independent judges also guard against the 'ill humors' of temporary majorities, moments when public passion pushes for laws that trample individuals. Someone has to hold the line until reflection returns.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 8: Even short of unconstitutional laws, unjust ones injure private citizens, and independent judges temper their damage. Knowing courts will scrutinize them also quietly deters bad laws from passing at all.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 9: The steadfastness this job requires cannot come from judges who need reappointment. Judges worrying about their next term will consult popularity instead of the Constitution.
Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton)|Passage 10: The wrap-up: lifetime tenure during good behavior, copied from the best existing models, is the wisest available design for an independent judiciary. The convention would have been at fault to omit it.
